“I mainly do like a blend between fry and false. With my highs I can do more nasally high screams.”
“I can do gutturals, and some people say my lows sound more like mids — that’s just my natural tone.”
"False cord: pushing air through the most relaxed state your throat can be in, it  sounds like a growl"
"Fry: Uses a different part of the throat. Not easy to describe. Sounds like someone crying out in pain—feels higher in the tract, a thinner, airy sound."
“That kind of yelly hardcore scream is more raw — you’re yelling and letting your vocal crack.”

“"Screaming" for me is umbrella: can be fry, false cord, or in between (in high/low register). Both fry and false cord can be low or high, so if you do false cord in low it's growling, in high—screaming; with fry, can do both.”
“I practice stamina a lot because my vocals are very loud and take a lot of power — breath control is super important for me.”
“Before shows I warm up by doing push-ups, chopped screams, and a five-minute YouTube vocal warm-up — just humming and getting things moving.”
“I drink water, warm up my body, and focus on breath control — as long as I can hear myself on stage, it keeps things safe and consistent.”
“Back in 2020 I’d get so light-headed from screaming wrong that I once saw fuzz and fell to the ground — but I got better after improving my technique and breath control.”
“Fry is fry screaming — they’re quieter, but at the end of the day they both sound similar on a microphone.”
“Growling is more like death-medley, deeper and more gurgly compared to fry.”
“Super easy for me (I figured it out first). Like a yelly/shouty vocal—a Brian Garris/Noctlus style. You can do it chesty, head voice, even falsetto.“

“Other types of cream: tunnel scream (false cord, tongue twisted), gutturals (toilety/gross sound, tongue all the way forward), tea kettle/whistle screams (hi-freq, high register), pig squeals (high register, but can be growly).“
“Oh yeah — every word I say, I want to pronounce it. I prefer to be able to pronounce things.”
“Enunciation is the biggest decider in intelligibility. I want to pronounce all my words as best as I can — that’s how I try to do it even when screaming.”
"Because they can't make sense of the lyrics, not used to the sounds, and also lack of clear enunciation/rhythm sometimes."

"Rhythm—I think it plays a lot to do with intelligibility.  My hypothesis: if a phrase is vocalized without rhythm, you can't just do something without rhythm. If a phrase is broken up with a more suited rhythm for the words, then it would be more intelligible—that is my hypothesis."
“If I see the band live, then I really like it — that always makes me connect to the music more.”
“I like that kind of high fry stuff — it helps get that anger out and just really express it.”

“Last year I was in four bands at once—playing shows almost every week, sometimes three shows a month, plus a tour.”

“I dated an opera vocal coach who taught me about glottal pressure—holding the throat structures in place and blowing through a ‘coffee straw’ so everything relaxed and I could sustain death metal vocals much longer.”
“Apart from that, I never really had formal extreme vocal tutoring—there was this ‘metal coach’ who did DVDs 20 years ago and I just saw some of her videos on YouTube, but that was it.”
“My learning style is using non-typical tools in atypical ways to build my own method—it’s not always the easy way, but I’m stubborn and like constructing my own way of doing things.”
“When I do old-school death metal I want it as cavemanny as possible—very Nathan Explosion, more into growling and guttural stuff, definitely more in that direction.”
“On long runs of shows, if my death metal vocals started getting hard to sustain, I’d switch to black metal screeching for a while, bouncing back and forth between the two just on a whim.”

“I learned John Gallagher’s approach—getting the vocal cords into a high-timbre position, then going low and forcing air through it to sustain the sound—which I think sounds really cool.”

“During the show I’ll literally be talking to the crowd, take a sip of coffee, put the cup down, and go straight into the next song—it just keeps my voice where it needs to be.”

So it started mainly because of my sister and my brother-in-law. They introduced me to some of the things that I listen to now. So it started mostly with like punk genre and kind of evolved into, hey, check out this band. And then I started listening to stuff like Korn, which is more like metal related.

And then as I went through high school, I started to develop my own tastes and discover new things within the genre. So eventually my music taste started getting like heavier and heavier, and I started to listen to a variety of things.
One of my inspirations that I feel like people probably wouldn't expect is I really like Lionel Richie. So I like The Commodores, and I also like his solo stuff.

But for metal, currently, it would be the band Boundaries. The vocalist, Matt McDougall, his lyricism is like one of the best I've seen so far. It's so raw and poetic at the same time. So that's a huge inspiration for me. And also I like his vocal style.

I like ambient records. So a lot of ambient instrumentals. There's one by this band called Loath. They're actually a metal band, but they also have like this whole album that's just ambience, and it features really nice, soft saxophone in one of the tracks. I feel like I'm being transported somewhere else. It's a beautiful track.

I mean, I have a guitar. I'm not the best at it, but I do have this MIDI keyboard I like to use for side projects because I also like to make ambient music. Other than that, it's pretty much just the MIDI keyboard.

Yeah. For the stuff that I'm currently working on, the solo stuff, I kind of just start with finding the right bass synth. And I just play around with chords that I like, feeling it out. For this recent track, I found three to four chords I liked, held them out and changed between for a minute and a half to two minutes.
So I start with that and it makes it easier to layer things on top versus starting with a blank canvas and leading with the melody. I like to start with the foundation.
For my digital audio workstation, I like to use Reaper. Some people prefer Logic Pro or FL Studio, but I just like the interface of Reaper much better. It seems less confusing.
I actually used to be in band. I played the trumpet in sixth grade all the way till ninth grade. And in ninth grade, I did marching band for a year.

We do metalcore. It's a fun genre because we get to pretty much do whatever we want. It's kind of an umbrella term nowadays. Mainly screams and stuff, but we have parts where our rhythm guitarist will do clean vocals too.
Yes. We actually played at the Masquerade the night before school classes started.
This month, we don't have shows, but I would say it's about maybe once a month.
We actually are working on our EP right now. It's being mixed and mastered, but I think we'll have our latest single ready to be released hopefully within this month, if not next month.

When I'm doing live shows, I feel like I'm a bit more loosened up. It's easier for me to warm up for screaming and stuff versus in the studio. It kind of takes me a minute to get where I need to get. I'm not sure why that is, but it just makes it easier to play live.

I started experimenting with that kind of stuff when I was like 17 and a half, but didn't take it fully serious until probably the start of my freshman year of college. That's when I started, like, okay, every day I'm going to practice my vocals to sound better and try to progress somewhere.

Primarily, it was YouTube and Reddit. My primary way of doing vocals is called false chords. So I learned that through YouTube and Reddit, mainly learning how to do it the safe way—because once you practice how to do it the safe way, you can pretty much do it on your own. Just consistent practice and building that muscle memory to feel it out, and from there it just got better and better as time went on.

When we're going to have a show, I typically go on vocal rest—try to talk as little as possible (very hard for me because I like to talk a lot). Also, only room temperature water, because cold water can cause my throat to tighten up. Sometimes tea with honey helps.
Same thing with recording. Also, on a day-to-day basis, I try not to scream unless I'm practicing and try to keep things consistent.

Yeah, but it's just like "caveman noises"—just grunting over and over again. I'd do it now, but the walls are kind of thin. That's my main way of warming up—I'll just hold that for 30 seconds and do it over and over again.
It's kind of raw sounding as I do false cords, so it's a little bit more edgier.
I want to learn fry screams, but I have had no luck trying to figure out how to do it. Tried for like a year and just could not get how people do it.
Our rhythm guitarist can do fry screams—he tried showing me. Still didn't get it. So it's primarily false chords that I do.

I definitely agree because some of them, you can have hybrid screams basically. When you do a growl, you can use a false chord style and also do a growl and that will sound different than if you were to use a fry scream and try to do the same growl.

It's all in how you're placing your tongue, if your tongue is arched, the shape of your mouth—all affects that. There are a million ways you can combine things together to get different kinds of sounds.
Yeah, I think that would be the easiest one to do. And screaming as well.

I'd say for me, yes. I want people to hear what I'm writing, and that's just part of my vocal style, too. Compared to a lot of the other stuff in the same genre, you can understand what I'm saying better—even though I am screaming, I am a lot more articulate.
That's just how I am; I can't try not to be less articulate. I've tried that before for certain parts, but I can't do it—it just comes out articulate no matter what.
Yeah, typically—because when I listen to music, I like to sit down and listen from an album from start to finish. I'll have the lyrics pulled up. Some is easier to understand than others.
With metalcore songs, typically it's a lot easier to understand, get a feel for what they're saying, versus deathcore where it's a lot more intense and unintelligible.
But if I'm really focused, I can understand what they're saying or pick out a few words. If I know the song, you can hear it more clearly once you know the lyrics.
Honestly, no, not really—it just kind of comes out that way.

Yeah, it exists to a certain degree—not in the same way as normal singing. But I do have lows, mid-range, and high that I do to add for variation. I think it's important because you don't want to be monotone.
My voice is kind of high, so I can't go too low, but I can go high, so I like to go from lower mid-range up high on certain parts.

Yeah, sometimes I'll go with the rhythm guitar—I write lyrics and have a vocal cadence specific to what the guitar is playing sometimes. Other times, I ignore the guitar and make my own rhythm, and it can still mesh correctly. But typically, I write to the rhythm of the guitar.
Yeah, I'd say so. I'll get a lot of grizzly bear or alligator; just some sort of primal animal like that.
I think it goes back to when humans, before speech, used false chords to intimidate animals, try to make ourselves seem bigger. So I think there's a relation.
Probably one of the Pantera songs from the '90s, like "Cowboys from Hell." There's definitely grit in their vocals—think that's where people started getting more extreme.
Probably early hardcore when they started to yell instead of sing. Metallica—singing with occasional yelling. In early hardcore, it was all just yelling.
Name vocalists, bands, artists that have influenced you:
I think my dad started playing—was taking guitar lessons when I was like eight or something. I guess I just thought it looked cool, and I started playing guitar.

I was always into rock and stuff. Growing up, I was into Led Zeppelin, ACDC, that kind of stuff. I got really into all those instrumental prog artists, like Intervals, Plini, Animals as Leaders, and that kind of area. I got really into that when I was in university, around 19, because I just didn't realize you could make this kind of music just with guitar, and I thought that was really sick. My taste kind of snowballed from there. Now I mainly listen just to metal. Before then, I think I was like, any music with screaming vocals—I was like, "I'm not into that."
Progressive metal is probably the main one I listen to—and kind of metalcore, deathcore—but mostly into progressive metal and the stuff around that.

I like the technicality—because I played guitar since I was a kid, it's fun to learn songs that are kind of difficult and technical. In general, I like music that has some interesting kind of vibe or weirdness, experimentation. I get bored with more traditional metal genres; they don't grip me the way prog and the genres around prog do.
Some of my favorite bands growing up were Pink Floyd and Rush, so I think I just evolved—anything that feels cinematic, vibey, psychedelic, that's what I tend to enjoy.

Yeah, but really it's only specific bands, like Humanity's Last Breath (not sure if it's truly deathcore—it's kind of its own thing), Vildjarda, sometimes early Bring Me the Horizon. But mainly if a deathcore band has something interesting.

Yeah, I like a lot of rock, indie rock, psychedelic rock, jam bands, bluesy stuff—usually centered around rock or rock band instrumentation. Big fan of Jack Johnson (that's its own thing), Tame Impala, jammy/experimental stuff, Pigeons Playing Ping Pong. But mainly, I listen to stuff within rock or indie umbrellas.
Guitar is my main instrument. I also play bass guitar, drums, and then I sing.

I usually write in a DAW (digital audio workstation). I'm the stereotype: use GetGood Drums, Neural DSP guitar tones, sometimes sample bass. I write riffs, drum suites, build out a demo, come up with vocals and record those on top. I try to fully produce a song in the DAW—I don't write tabs for my guitar parts, just come up with ideas, record them. If I want to turn it into an album, I'll hire a drummer and rerecord everything. I use a writing template: drum mix, master chain, guitar tones already set. All done in Reaper ("awesome"/super customizable/stable—way better workflow for me than other DAWs).
Not really. I did band in high school, played saxophone, that's the closest. I've also taken some guitar lessons, including online guitar lessons with a teacher (Sean Ash, instrumental guitarist).
Not really. I have a solo project, but I'm not explicitly in a band. The thing is just me making music.
I was in a jam band at university (played guitar).
These days—never. I did play recently at a conference (Izmir); they do a yearly jam, and last year we did Master of Puppets, there was a mosh pit.

Started by taking a couple of online lessons, found a website that explained fry scream (wanted to sound like Spencer from Periphery). Took a few lessons to get basics; then practiced, recorded stuff. So, yes, very explicitly "screaming lessons".

Yeah, a bit. I start by doing vocal fry, stuff to get my voice warmed up. I mash together various YouTube video routines (lots of growl-like warmups, scales, arpeggios). I always do a "five-minute vocal warmup" kind of video, then I sing songs—starting from soft clean singing (like Contortionist's "Clairvoyant") and build up intensity to doing screams.

Not really. If I try to scream two or three days in a row, by the second or third day I won't have the scream anymore, throat feels a bit off—so I space it out. But the more regularly I do it, the longer sessions I can do. Haven't had "legit" health issues; just sometimes need to back off if I feel a bit sore.
I pretty much only know fry scream (or what I think is the fry scream).
I would compare it to Meshuggah—the vocalist from Meshuggah is probably closest to how I sound when I scream.

Initially aimed for the Spencer Sotelo (Periphery) type sound, but realized I don't have that range. Modeled my scream on Meshuggah and Between the Buried and Me—practiced covers of those bands, their screams have less range, easy to home in on sound. Now I've been able to expand—doing more low and high screams.
Michael Lassard (The Contortionist, for clean singing and older screams)
Main focus is on fry scream (all the focus of my screaming).

Recently worked more on expanding range (lows and highs), found mouth posture/shape important for achieving lows/highs. Not focused on learning more types right now but would be interested in guttural/deeper types in future.
(Interested in): technique for switching from singing to screaming (e.g., what Spencer or Courtney LaPlante do), but haven't mastered
High fry, low fry, etc. (variation via posture/pitch)

Not super aware of exact difference between a growl and my scream. Sometimes people describe my low screams as growls, but I use the same technique—just change pitch/timbre. Growling seems like a lower, bassier sound; screaming is higher pitched. Both achieved by same fundamental technique for me. "Growl" isn't a specific technique, but more an umbrella or perceptual class.

Yelling/shouting (associated with hardcore) feels completely different from fry scream—different vocal projection, much louder. Can't bridge gap between singing and screaming for this reason. Took a lesson on false cord screaming—could help bridge that gap, but haven't mastered it.
Not really—I don't think I would be able to always tell (maybe a more attuned ear could). Personally, false cord screams I've heard don't sound very different from mine.
To some extent, but it's less important than for clean singing.

In general, I don't always pay much attention to lyrics—maybe because I'm a musician. Part of the appeal of extreme vocals is the intensity—it fits the musical passage/emotion. Lyrics can elevate, but I don't care that much if I can't understand the lyrics. That said, I tend to listen to music with more intelligible screaming (not pure unintelligible death metal). I like the intensity and natural, human variability—same reason I like distorted guitars.

A lot of people do care. When I talk to people who don't listen to metal, not understanding lyrics is the main thing they gripe about—"just screaming/can't even understand what they're saying." It's the number one turn-off for non-metal listeners.
Many people (especially non-musicians) focus on vocals/lyrics in music; I'm more focused on music/emotion.

It depends. "Most of the time metal vocals are very melody-less." Bands like Architects do melodic screaming—it fits their poppier, catchy style. Bands like Meshuggah—no melody in vocals, fits their approach. Melody may require less distortion, potentially affecting intelligibility.

Maybe a bit. Intelligibility depends more on style, singing technique, and processing (modern metal often has highly compressed vocals, which can help). In bands with extreme rhythms (Meshuggah, Between the Buried and Me), it can distract from lyrics—one listens to the rhythm rather than the words.
Modern history: Beatles—"Helter Skelter," or "I Want You (She's So Heavy)"—loud, distorted, yelling. It's not exactly modern screaming technique, but it's what comes to mind.
Other bands like ACDC, Led Zeppelin in the 70s/80s did "distorted singing" when pushing for more volume/intensity.

For growl: "We use growl to describe vocals and also to describe animal sounds." Words like growl, screech come from animal noises—pig squeal is an explicit animal reference. It's natural: lions roar (aggression, danger)—very "metal" associations. So yes, animals are a good way to describe extreme vocals.
